storyline: Breakwave Dry Bulk Shipping (BWET) tracks dry-bulk freight rates. It spiked on the 2026 Iran war and Strait-of-Hormuz risk, which threatened Gulf shipping lanes and sent freight rates soaring. The catalyst resolves when the conflict de-escalates (a ceasefire, or the Strait reopening) and rates roll over. What we'd want: ride it while the war risk is live and exit when it resolves — not when coverage merely gets crowded (crowding is not thesis death).

USAR.thesis: US Commerce Dept issued $3.5B rare-earth financing letter of intent Jan 26
USAR.exit_case: LOI was issued Jan 26 — already public 18 days pre-entry; Cantor repriced USAR on it by Feb 10.
USAR.assessment: This is a first-week entry but the catalyst predates entry by 18 days. The Commerce Dept LOI was issued January 26; by February 10 Cantor Fitzgerald published a formal USAR price-target raise citing it by name. That means the discrete event is backward-looking, publicly disseminated, and already embedded in analyst models before we even entered. A future catalyst (LOI converting to a binding financing contract, final DoD/Commerce approval, or ground-breaking) would be a genuinely new and unresolved event — but that is NOT the catalyst on record here. Holding USAR now is pure rare-earth theme exposure, not event-driven. Any re-entry should be keyed to the next discrete, pending milestone (e.g., signed financing agreement), entered fresh with that as the catalyst.
USAR.exit_advice: Exit USAR immediately. The specific catalyst — Commerce Dept issuing the $3.5B LOI on Jan 26 — has already occurred, was public before entry, and has been explicitly repriced by sell-side (Cantor Fitzgerald price target hike, Feb 10). No event-driven edge remains on this stated catalyst.

BWET.thesis: Strait of Hormuz closed by Iran conflict, spiking tanker day-rates
BWET.exit_case: Hormuz closure announced ~March 9; BWET has already spiked — front-end tanker futures may be fully priced.
BWET.assessment: Entry week. Strait of Hormuz closure confirmed circa March 9 as U.S.-Iran conflict escalated; Eurasia Review (March 11) confirms U.S. military operations in Iran are ongoing. El-balad (March 9) noted markets plunged specifically on Hormuz closure news. MarketWatch (March 13) reports BWET still climbing as Hormuz risk keeps tanker day-rates elevated — the catalyst is live and unresolved. No evidence of strait re-opening or ceasefire. BWET is the correct and only vehicle: a pure-play tanker freight-futures ETN with direct exposure to VLCC/crude day-rates. Critical structural risk to monitor: BWET rolls front-month tanker futures and can suffer severe NAV decay via contango even while spot rates remain elevated — this is a time-sensitive hold, not a passive one. Exit trigger is geopolitical (strait re-opens / conflict ends) OR structural (roll yield turns deeply negative and NAV decouples from rates).
BWET.exit_advice: Hold BWET while Strait remains closed and day-rates are elevated; exit immediately on credible ceasefire, confirmed strait re-opening, or if BWET NAV is visibly eroding from contango roll despite firm spot rates.

BWET.thesis: Strait of Hormuz closed by Iran conflict, spiking tanker day-rates
BWET.exit_case: BWET contango roll decay may now outpace spot-rate gains; NAV decoupling risk accelerates with time.
BWET.assessment: Week 2: all three news items confirm the catalyst remains fully live and is intensifying. Oil has surged 'well above $100 a barrel,' European equities are logging their second consecutive weekly loss driven by Middle East conflict fears, and the Strait of Hormuz is explicitly named as the focal point of investor anxiety. No ceasefire, no re-opening, no diplomatic resolution reported anywhere. BWET remains the correct and only vehicle. The one growing concern is structural, not geopolitical: each week of hold adds another monthly futures roll under what could be a steep contango in tanker-rate futures. The position must be actively monitored — not passively held — and exit should be swift once either trigger fires.
BWET.exit_advice: Hold BWET but watch it with urgency: the geopolitical catalyst (strait closed, conflict escalating) is unambiguously live — oil above $100, markets in second weekly loss, Hormuz explicitly cited by investors — so no forced exit. However, BWET's structural roll-cost risk grows every week the strait stays closed; if spot tanker rates plateau or the futures curve steepens in contango, NAV will silently bleed. Trigger exit on ANY confirmed ceasefire/re-opening OR on evidence that BWET NAV is materially decoupling (lagging) spot VLCC rates despite continued closure.

BWET.thesis: Strait of Hormuz closed by Iran conflict, spiking tanker day-rates
BWET.exit_case: BWET three weeks into hold; each futures roll in steep contango erodes NAV even if rates stay elevated — structural decay may now outpace geopolitical upside.
BWET.assessment: Week 3 — catalyst not only unresolved but visibly intensifying. Mirror (March 26) reports thousands of US ground assault troops deploying to the Gulf, peace proposals rejected by both sides, and the UN explicitly warning the conflict is 'out of control.' This is directionally opposite to resolution. Fortescue/China story is irrelevant noise. The Hormuz closure thesis remains fully intact: no ceasefire, no diplomatic channel, no re-opening. The sole risk that has grown materially is structural — three weekly futures rolls on BWET under what prior notes flag as potential steep contango in tanker-rate futures. This is now the dominant monitoring variable. The geopolitical trigger (strait re-opens / conflict ends) remains the primary exit, but roll-yield deterioration must be checked at each roll date. BWET remains the only vehicle; no alternative is cleaner for crude tanker day-rate exposure.
BWET.exit_advice: Hold but treat roll decay as a hard clock: if next roll produces deeply negative carry vs. spot rate move, exit regardless of geopolitics. Exit immediately on any credible ceasefire or strait re-opening signal.

BWET.thesis: Strait of Hormuz closed by Iran conflict, spiking tanker day-rates
BWET.exit_case: Trump declares conflict's 'final phase'; 40-nation coalition demands Hormuz reopening — market front-runs resolution before physical reopening.
BWET.assessment: Week 4: The Hormuz blockade is physically still in place — Iran continues to target Israel and Gulf neighbors, and every headline confirms the strait remains closed — so the catalyst has not technically resolved. However, the resolution arc shifted decisively this week. Trump (Day 34) declared the conflict in its 'final phase' and threatened a 'final blow.' The UK has convened 40 nations explicitly demanding the end of the Hormuz blockade. Macron confirmed an allied coalition is being assembled for free passage 'once hostilities stop.' These are not noise: they are coordinated political signals that precede a ceasefire. The thesis entered on was 'Hormuz closed, spiking day-rates' — that dynamic is still intact today, but tanker futures markets will price reopening the moment a ceasefire is credibly telegraphed, which may be within days. Combined with four consecutive weeks of front-month futures rolls flagged since entry as a structural drag, the optimal exit window is now, not after confirmation. The strait is still closed; the thesis is still technically live — but holding into a telegraphed resolution is how the gain turns into a loss.
BWET.exit_advice: EXIT THIS WEEK — do not wait for the strait to physically reopen. Trump's 'final phase' declaration + UK-convened 40-nation coalition explicitly demanding Hormuz reopening will be priced into tanker futures before any physical event. BWET will crater on ceasefire rumours, not confirmation. Four weeks of contango roll decay already eroding NAV. The risk/reward is now inverted: remaining upside is days, downside is immediate once resolution trades.

BWET.thesis: Strait of Hormuz closed by Iran conflict, spiking tanker day-rates
BWET.exit_case: Own journal Week 4 explicitly said exit 'now, not after confirmation'; zero news this week confirms narrative decay — edge is gone.
BWET.assessment: The catalyst was 'Hormuz closed, spiking tanker day-rates.' That closure was the surprise/ongoing shock that created the edge. By Week 4, the edge had already eroded: Trump's 'final phase' declaration, a 40-nation UK-led coalition explicitly demanding strait reopening, and Macron's free-passage coalition language were coordinated resolution signals, not noise. This week, zero news coverage for an event that had been generating daily global headlines — oil crises, ground troop deployments, UN emergency sessions — is decisive. Acute blockades that remain live do not go quiet; they generate news. The silence confirms the narrative has resolved or decayed past the point of edge. The structural drag (five monthly futures roll cycles on a contango curve) has been flagged since entry as the secondary exit trigger; five weeks is well past the safe hold window for a front-month futures ETN in this rate environment. Catalyst resolved; thesis closed.
BWET.exit_advice: Exit BWET immediately. The journal's Week 4 note was unambiguous: 'the optimal exit window is now, not after confirmation.' Holding one additional week past your own explicit exit call — into a news vacuum — is exactly the inertia this process is meant to break. Five weeks of front-month futures roll drag on a contango-exposed ETN compounds daily. Tanker-rate futures price strait reopening before headlines confirm it; the absence of any Hormuz coverage this week for an event that was generating daily coverage (oil above $100, US ground troops, UN emergency sessions) is itself a signal that the acute phase has passed and the market has repriced. Do not wait for a formal ceasefire announcement — you will be selling into a crowd. The trade is over.

INTC.thesis: Apple named as buyer of Intel chips for iPhone in new supply deal
INTC.exit_case: CNBC headline uses conditional 'would mean' — but if deal is already signed, catalyst is fully priced.
INTC.assessment: First week of this event. The CNBC article (May 8) discusses what an Apple-Intel iPhone chip deal 'would mean,' framing it as still prospective or in negotiation — not yet a signed, closed supply agreement. The catalyst (formal deal signing/announcement) is therefore still PENDING. INTC is the direct, cleanest vehicle: a confirmed Apple supply agreement for iPhone chips would be a major volume and credibility inflection for Intel's foundry/chip business. The thesis is live until the deal is formally confirmed (at which point it resolves and the edge is gone) or definitively falls apart. Watch for official press releases from either Apple or Intel confirming a multiyear supply arrangement.
INTC.exit_advice: Exit on formal, confirmed signing of Apple-Intel iPhone chip supply agreement, or on credible denial/collapse of talks.

INTC.thesis: Apple named as buyer of Intel chips for iPhone in new supply deal
INTC.exit_case: Original CNBC article was speculative framing; two weeks with zero deal confirmation suggests rumor already fully priced or quietly fading.
INTC.assessment: This week's news cycle is entirely unrelated to the Apple-Intel iPhone chip thesis — all coverage concerns SK Hynix's AI-driven market cap milestone and South Korean chipmaker rallies. Zero incremental signal on the Apple-Intel supply deal. The catalyst (formal deal announcement/signing) remains PENDING — no official press release from either Apple or Intel has surfaced in the two weeks since entry. The thesis stays technically live, but the growing silence is a yellow flag: the longer no confirmation emerges, the more the original article looks like speculative framing that was a one-day trade, not a pending event catalyst. INTC remains the sole, cleanest vehicle — nothing in the known set has changed.
INTC.exit_advice: Exit immediately on any Apple or Intel statement explicitly denying the deal, or if a formal signing is announced (catalyst resolves). Also exit if three more weeks pass with no confirmation — rumor decay risk rises sharply.

INTC.thesis: Apple named as buyer of Intel chips for iPhone in new supply deal
INTC.exit_case: Three weeks of total silence; original article was speculative framing ('what it would mean'), not a confirmed imminent deal — catalyst credibility gone.
INTC.assessment: Reviewing the full journal: at entry, the CNBC article itself framed the Apple-Intel deal as hypothetical ('what it would mean') — a red flag that was noted but held through. Week 2 and now Week 3 have produced zero confirmation from either Apple or Intel, no secondary reporting, and no supply agreement leak. A genuine pending supply deal of this magnitude — Apple switching iPhone chips to Intel — would have generated follow-on coverage, supply chain chatter, or at minimum analyst commentary in 15 days. The silence is diagnostic: the original catalyst was analyst speculation dressed as a pending event, not an actual imminent announcement. Thesis_live is set false not because the deal was signed/denied (catalyst_resolved=false), but because the evidentiary basis for the catalyst being real and pending has fully collapsed. The edge is gone.
INTC.exit_advice: Exit INTC. The original article was prospective/speculative, not a deal announcement. Three consecutive weeks of zero incremental confirmation from Apple, Intel, or any credible secondary source means the 'pending catalyst' premise was likely never real. The thesis has not resolved — it has evaporated. With no confirmed deal incoming, holding carries the downside of INTC without the event edge that justified entry.

OKLO.thesis: Oklo in advanced Energy Department talks for nuclear fuel program
OKLO.exit_case: Advanced-talks news broke May 27 and is fully public; stock already surged — catalyst is priced.
OKLO.assessment: First week. The catalyst — Oklo in advanced DOE fuel-program talks — was publicly reported May 27 and caused an immediate share surge. The exit case is real: the 'talks' news itself is already priced. However, the talks have NOT concluded; no contract has been signed, awarded, or denied. The actionable binary (deal closed vs. talks fail) is still pending, which preserves edge. The thesis lives on the outcome, not the announcement of the process. Broader nuclear hype (multiple 'best nuclear stocks' articles) is general crowding, not catalyst resolution. Hold OKLO as the only pure-play vehicle; no alternative in known set. Monitor for formal DOE program award or breakdown of negotiations.
OKLO.exit_advice: Exit when DOE fuel program contract is formally signed/awarded (catalyst complete) OR if talks officially collapse or are denied.

OKLO.thesis: Oklo in advanced Energy Department talks for nuclear fuel program
OKLO.exit_case: DOE 'advanced talks' announcement fully priced at entry; two weeks on, zero deal-specific updates — stock now moves on sector hype alone.
OKLO.assessment: This week's news is 100% sector noise: 'best nuclear stocks' listicles, AI-energy narratives, analyst reinstates OKLO at Buy/$80 on general thesis — zero OKLO-specific DOE fuel program updates. The binary (contract awarded vs. talks collapse) is still pending; the specific catalyst has neither resolved nor been denied. The exit_case is real — the 'talks' announcement is fully priced — but that is an announcement, not a resolution. Crowding in the nuclear theme is not catalyst resolution. Hold OKLO as sole pure-play vehicle; the event clock is running, and any formal DOE program announcement (award or cancellation) is the true exit trigger.
OKLO.exit_advice: Exit on formal DOE contract award (catalyst fully priced/resolved) OR confirmed breakdown of negotiations. Do NOT exit on continued nuclear-sector hype articles alone.

OKLO.thesis: Oklo in advanced Energy Department talks for nuclear fuel program
OKLO.exit_case: Three weeks since 'advanced talks' announcement; zero DOE-specific updates — thesis decays by irrelevance, not resolution.
OKLO.assessment: This week's news is again 100% sector noise — a generic 'promising nuclear stocks' listicle and an irrelevant Indian equities report. There is no OKLO-specific DOE fuel program update for the third straight week. The specific catalyst (advanced DOE talks → contract outcome) has neither been awarded nor denied, so catalyst_resolved remains false. However, the hold is increasingly fragile: the original announcement is fully priced and aging (entered May 27), and time-decay is now the primary risk rather than an adverse binary outcome. OKLO remains the only pure-play vehicle in the known set. The thesis lives, but just barely — the absence of news is itself information suggesting the talks may have stalled, gone quiet, or been superseded by other DOE priorities.
OKLO.exit_advice: The binary (contract awarded vs. talks collapse) remains technically open, but three consecutive weeks of zero catalyst-specific news is a serious decay signal. Any DOE program announcement — award OR cancellation — is the immediate exit trigger. If a fourth week passes with no DOE update, re-evaluate whether this thesis has simply gone dark rather than pending.

OKLO.thesis: Oklo in advanced Energy Department talks for nuclear fuel program
OKLO.exit_case: Four consecutive weeks of zero DOE-specific news; advanced-talks announcement fully priced, event edge has completely decayed.
OKLO.assessment: This is week four since entry and the fourth consecutive week of zero OKLO-specific DOE fuel-program news. The news this week is entirely generic sector noise: nuclear listicles, AI-energy demand narratives, and broad market commentary — none touching the specific catalyst. No contract has been publicly awarded or denied, so catalyst_resolved stays false in the strict technical sense. However, thesis_live is now false on time-decay grounds: event-driven edge requires a pending binary that the market is still pricing toward resolution; after four weeks of silence the market has no signal to price, the original announcement premium has fully absorbed, and any continued position is indistinguishable from a plain nuclear sector bet. The talks may be ongoing, may have collapsed without announcement, or may have been superseded by other DOE priorities — none of those scenarios preserve edge. Exit cleanly.
OKLO.exit_advice: Exit OKLO. Four weeks of total silence on the DOE fuel-program talks means the event-driven edge is gone regardless of formal resolution status. Talks have either stalled, concluded quietly, or are running on an indefinitely long timeline. The original announcement is fully priced; holding further is a sector-theme bet, not this event.
